title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
description: Cross-Site Scripting allows attackers to inject malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, and malicious redirects.
learning_objectives: Distinguish between reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS
Identify where untrusted data enters an HTML page unsafely
Apply context-aware output encoding to neutralise injected scripts
Implement Content Security Policy as a defence layer
Recognise XSS vulnerabilities in AI-generated template and rendering code

sections.title: How XSS Works
sections.content: XSS occurs when an application takes untrusted input and writes it into the HTML of a page without properly encoding it first. The browser receives what looks like legitimate HTML — containing embedded script tags or event handlers — and executes the malicious code with the same trust level as the legitimate page. Unlike SQL injection which targets the server, XSS targets the users visiting the application.
sections.key_concept: The browser cannot tell the difference between the developer's intended HTML and an attacker's injected script — both arrive in the same page response.

types.name: Reflected XSS
types.description: The malicious script is embedded in a URL and reflected immediately in the page response. The victim must be tricked into clicking a crafted link. The script is not stored on the server.

types.name: Stored XSS
types.description: The malicious script is saved to the server — in a database, comment field, or user profile — and later rendered to all users who view that content. This is the most dangerous type as it affects every visitor without any interaction from the attacker.

types.name: DOM-Based XSS
types.description: The vulnerability exists entirely in client-side JavaScript. The page reads attacker-controlled data (from the URL, cookies, or localStorage) and writes it directly into the DOM using innerHTML or document.write — the server is never involved.

sections.title: Real-World Attack Scenario
sections.scenario: A student forum allows users to post comments. The developer renders comment text directly into the HTML template without encoding. An attacker posts a comment containing a script tag that silently sends every visitor's session cookie to an attacker-controlled server. Every student who views the thread has their session stolen — the attacker can then impersonate them without knowing their password.
impact: Session hijacking — steal authentication cookies and impersonate users
Credential harvesting — inject fake login forms that submit to attacker servers
Keylogging — capture everything the user types on the page
Defacement — modify the visible content of the page
Malware distribution — redirect users to pages hosting drive-by downloads

vulnerable.code:
# VULNERABLE: rendering raw user input in HTML # In Flask route: @app.route('/profile') def profile(): name = request.args.get('name') return f'

Hello, {name}!

' # Attacker URL: /profile?name= # The script executes in the victim's browser
vulnerable.problem: The f-string directly embeds user input into HTML. The browser interprets the injected script tag as legitimate code.

secure.code:
# SECURE: use Jinja2 auto-escaping (enabled by default) # In template (templates/profile.html): #

Hello, {{ name }}!

# Double curly braces auto-escape special characters # Or escape manually: from markupsafe import escape @app.route('/profile') def profile(): name = request.args.get('name') return f'

Hello, {escape(name)}!

' #
secure.fix: escape() converts < > & " ' into their HTML entity equivalents. The browser renders them as visible characters rather than interpreting them as HTML or script.

vulnerable.problem: innerHTML parses its argument as HTML, so any tags or event handlers in the string are rendered and executed.

secure.fix: textContent assigns plain text. No HTML is parsed. Script tags and event handlers are displayed as visible characters, not executed.

sections.title: Security-by-Design Principles
sections.content: Preventing XSS requires encoding output in the right context. The same character may be safe in one context and dangerous in another.

principles.title: Use a Templating Engine with Auto-Escaping
principles.detail: Modern templating engines like Jinja2, Handlebars, and Blade auto-escape variables by default. Never disable auto-escaping unless you are deliberately rendering trusted HTML.

principles.title: Content Security Policy (CSP)
principles.detail: CSP is an HTTP response header that tells the browser which sources of scripts, styles, and other resources are trusted. A strict CSP prevents injected scripts from executing even if XSS is present.

principles.title: HttpOnly and Secure Cookie Flags
principles.detail: Setting the HttpOnly flag on session cookies prevents JavaScript from accessing them — meaning even a successful XSS attack cannot steal the session cookie via document.cookie.
principles.example:
# Flask session cookie configuration
app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY'] = True
app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE'] = True   # HTTPS only
app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE'] = 'Lax'

items.text: Always encode output before rendering in HTML, JavaScript, CSS, or URL contexts
Use a templating engine with auto-escaping enabled (never disable it for user content)
Use textContent instead of innerHTML when inserting text into the DOM
Implement a Content Security Policy header on all responses
Set HttpOnly and Secure flags on all session cookies
Validate input on the server side — reject unexpected content types or formats

sections.title: Watch Out in AI-Generated Code
sections.content: AI assistants commonly generate JavaScript that uses innerHTML to display dynamic content, and Python code that builds HTML strings with f-strings or string concatenation. Both patterns are XSS vulnerabilities when the data source is user-controlled.
red_flags: element.innerHTML = userInput — writing user data into innerHTML
document.write(variable) — writing user data directly to the document
return f'<tag>{variable}</tag>' — Python f-string building HTML
Jinja2 templates with {{ variable|safe }} applied to user-supplied content

quiz.question: A developer writes: element.innerHTML = req.query.name. What type of vulnerability does this introduce?
quiz.explanation: Writing user-controlled input into innerHTML is DOM-Based XSS. The vulnerability exists entirely in client-side JavaScript — the server is not involved. The browser parses the injected string as HTML, executing any embedded scripts or event handlers.

quiz.question: Which type of XSS is most dangerous and why?
options: Reflected XSS, because it executes on the server
DOM-based XSS, because it cannot be detected by server-side scanning
Stored XSS, because it executes for every user who views the affected page
quiz.explanation: Stored XSS is generally considered most dangerous because the malicious script is saved to the server and executes automatically for every user who views the affected content — no interaction from the attacker is required after the initial injection.

quiz.question: Which of the following correctly prevents XSS when displaying a user-supplied comment in HTML?
options: Remove all spaces from the input before rendering
Use escape(comment) or a templating engine that auto-escapes output
Wrap the output in a <div> tag
Check that the input length is less than 500 characters
quiz.explanation: Escaping converts dangerous characters like < > & into their HTML entity equivalents (e.g. <) so the browser renders them as visible text rather than interpreting them as HTML. Length checks and div wrappers do not prevent script injection.
